title: The Warning at the Table
subtitle: Christ, troubled in spirit, names the traitor among His own — and Judas goes out

stillness: Be still now, and grieving, and sorrowful.
Let this be the troubling of a Christian — not by misery, but by mercy.
gospel: And while they were eating, He said:
"Amen, I say to you, that one of you twelve will betray Me."
"Behold, the hand of him who betrays Me is with Me on the table."
This word entered into their hearts like a sharp sword.
And being saddened, they ceased to eat,
and looked upon one another, struck with a strange amazement,
And greatly saddened, they began each to say, every one for himself:
"Is it I, Lord — who shall betray You?"
"Woe to that man by whom the Son of Man shall be betrayed!"
behold: Behold the Lord Jesus, troubled in spirit — not in the flesh.
He troubled Himself, by His own power, with this proportioned distress.
It is a virtuous thing to be troubled against vice.
He is troubled mercifully: in compassion for the disciple who betrays Him,
and who in betraying Him perishes.
Behold the wondrous kindness of Christ toward a hardened disciple,
of the Lord toward the most wicked of servants.
He tries to draw the traitor back from so great a crime in four ways:
by shame — "the hand that betrays Me is with Me on the table";
by fear — "Woe to that man by whom the Son of Man is betrayed";
by the showing of kindness — He receives him at His own table, and washes his feet;
by the showing of affection, in gentle address — "Friend, why have you come?"
And yet Judas is broken by none of these,
that he might not betray the One he knew to be the Son of God.
consider: To one who wishes to ponder the Passion devoutly, the traitor's treachery meets him first.
He was so filled with the venom of fraud, that he betrayed his Master and Lord.
He was kindled with so great a flame of greed, that he sold God for silver,
and weighed the precious blood of Christ against the price of a vile reward.
He was of such ingratitude, that the One who had entrusted all things to him,
and raised him to the height of apostolic honor — him he sold to death.
He was of such hardness, that neither the intimacy of the banquet,
nor the humility of the foot-washing, nor the sweetness of the conversation,
nor the tenderness of the kiss, could call him back from his conceived malice.
Yet the dove-like meekness of the Lamb of God surpasses, beyond measure, the traitor's impiety.
And the eleven knew they had plotted no such thing against the Lord;
yet they believed their Master more than themselves,
and fearing their own frailty, sorrowful, they ask about a sin they are not conscious of.
"Who understands his faults?" — Let each one, too, fear for himself.
prayer: who willed in many ways to call Your disciple back from his errors to repentance,
call me back from all my wanderings and from all my errors.
Give me, my Savior, the power and the strength to conquer the devil,
who keeps watch continually and wars against me,
lest by his most wicked deceit he drag me to the pit of darkness;
but, upheld by Your help, may I come to the brightness of glory,
where the just, made radiant in the likeness of the angelic company,
shall shine as the sun, in the kingdom of their Father. Amen.

latinJewel.latin: Ó mira benignitas Christi in discipulum durum, Domini in servum nequissimum!
latinJewel.english: O wondrous kindness of Christ toward a hardened disciple, of the Lord toward the most wicked of servants!
